Swiss CitiesGuarda

Guarda

GRGraubünden

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The bell that chose Ursli

Retelling from the perspective of the big cowbell waiting alone in the dark mountain hut, hoping someone brave enough will come to find it. When young Ursli treks through deep snow to the Maiensass, the bell knows it has found its partner — and together they will lead the Chalandamarz procession with the biggest sound of all.

Create This Story

Schellen-Ursli (A Bell for Ursli), published in 1945, was written by Selina Chönz and illustrated by Alois Carigiet, who used Guarda as the setting. The story tells of young Ursli, given the smallest cowbell for the Chalandamarz procession, who treks alone to his family's mountain hut to retrieve a large bell. The book has sold an estimated 1.7 million copies in ten languages. Carigiet received the inaugural Hans Christian Andersen Award for illustration in 1966.

The sgraffito dragon

A child in Guarda discovers that the mythological dragon scratched into a house facade comes alive at night and guards the village from winter storms. The sgraffito technique — scratching designs through wet plaster to reveal the dark layer beneath — holds centuries of magic in every line carved by Renaissance artisans.

Create This Story

Sgraffito (from Italian "sgraffiare," to scratch) is a decorative technique brought to Graubünden by Renaissance master builders from Italy in the 16th century. A dark lime mortar layer is covered with a light coat; while still damp (about two hours), artisans scratch ornamental designs through it. Popular motifs include dragons, mermaids, rosettes, and heraldic symbols. Guarda is considered one of the finest concentrations of sgraffito in the Engadin, with over 60 historic houses preserved.

Chalandamarz morning

A shy child who has never dared to ring a bell joins the March 1st procession for the first time. Dressed in the blue Chalandamarz shirt and red scarf, ringing cowbells and cracking whips through the village streets, they discover that even the smallest bell can drive away winter and wake the good spirits of spring.

Create This Story

Chalandamarz ("first day of March") is celebrated on March 1st across the Romansh-speaking Engadin. Children dress in traditional blue shirts and red scarves and parade through the village ringing cowbells of different sizes, cracking whips, and singing Romansh songs to drive away the evil spirits of winter. The oldest child leads with the largest bell. Afterward, children collect donations of food and money for school trips. The tradition has roots reaching back to Roman times.

The house that remembered

An old Engadin chesa with walls so thick they hold centuries of memories whispers stories from the Suler to a child who presses an ear to the stone. Through the great arched entrance portal, past the kitchen and stable, the house reveals the lives of all the families who sheltered within its walls since the 16th century.

Create This Story

The traditional Engadin house (chesa) features thick walls built from wooden beams clad in stone, with small deep-set windows and stables beneath the living quarters for warmth. The defining feature is the Suler — a large arched entrance portal wide enough for a hay cart. Many houses bear carved inscriptions in Romansh with blessings and dates. Guarda has never suffered a catastrophic fire, so its 16th- and 17th-century houses survive intact — a living time capsule.

Lost in Val Tuoi

A child follows a marmot's whistle up into Val Tuoi and must find the way back to Guarda before darkness falls. Guided by the sound of village bells echoing up from far below, the child discovers the wild beauty of the Silvretta mountains and the comfort of knowing that home is always within earshot.

Create This Story

From Guarda, a path leads into the Val Tuoi, a dramatic side valley reaching into the Silvretta range. At its head, at 2,250 m, stands the Chamonna Tuoi (Tuoi Hut), a Swiss Alpine Club mountain hut reachable in about 2.5 hours on foot. From there, experienced mountaineers can summit Piz Buin (3,312 m). Guarda itself sits at 1,653 meters, over 300 meters above the Inn river, with a population of just 190 residents.

The architect's daughter

Set during Iachen Ulrich Könz's 1939–1945 restoration of Guarda: a girl helps her father carefully scratch new sgraffito onto a freshly plastered wall, learning that beauty takes patience. The careful work of preservation becomes a lesson in how old things can be kept alive if someone cares enough to restore them with love.

Create This Story

Between 1939 and 1945, architect Iachen Ulrich Könz (1899–1980), trained at ETH Zurich and Stuttgart, conducted a major restoration of Guarda's historic houses, preserving their authentic Engadin character. In 1939, Könz married Selina Chönz, who would write Schellen-Ursli. His meticulous work laid the foundation for Guarda's 1975 Wakker Prize from the Swiss Heritage Society, confirming it as one of the best-preserved villages in the Engadin.

Allegra! The last Romansh word

In a future where fewer and fewer people speak Romansh, a child in Guarda discovers a magical dictionary that brings forgotten words back to life as real things — "En" summons a river, "Chalandamarz" summons spring, "Allegra" summons a friend. The child must speak the words aloud to keep them from disappearing forever.

Create This Story

Guarda is a Romansh-speaking village where the dialect Vallader is spoken. Romansh descends from the Latin vernacular of the Roman conquest of Rhaetia in 15 BC. It became a national language in 1938 and an official language in 1996, but has only about 60,000 speakers (0.5% of the Swiss population, down from 1.1% in 1910). Each of the five Romansh idioms has a 400-year literary tradition. Common greetings include "Allegra!" (hello) and "Bun di" (good day).

The park where nothing is allowed

A child sneaks into the Swiss National Park expecting adventure but learns that the greatest magic is watching nature heal itself when humans simply step aside. An ibex on a cliff, a bearded vulture soaring overhead, marmots whistling warnings — in the oldest national park in the Alps, doing nothing is the bravest thing of all.

Create This Story

The Swiss National Park, founded on August 1, 1914, is the oldest national park in the Alps and Central Europe. Located near Zernez, a short distance from Guarda, it covers approximately 170 square kilometers of strictly protected wilderness (IUCN Category Ia) and is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. It was created in response to environmental degradation: the last lynx was spotted in 1876. Today it hosts ibex, chamois, marmots, golden eagles, and reintroduced bearded vultures.
